Details: Chromium (III) ions are too small to be retained by the filter.  Membrane filtration requires chromium (III) to be adsorbed or precipitated with a filterable particulate. Two examples may include a water-soluble macroligand (polyethlene-imine) and r-Al2O3 nanoparticles. Research has also shown that the iron hydroxide (RCF) process can also effectively remove chromium (III) from water. Studies indicated that membrane filtration can be used for chromium (III) removal (up to 100 percent). The high percent removals were due to the filtration of chromium (III) adsorbed on nano-particles or macroligands. Removal of chromium (III) ions using membrane filtration is achieved by employing a combined adsorption-ultrafiltration parallel process at pH range of 2 to 7.
